coredata.dc:description: As a vital biomarker, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is closely associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), whose ultrasensitive determination is essential to early diagnosis of HCC and sequential reduction of morbidity and mortality. Especially, the quantitative detection of AFP with high accuracy and ultra-sensitivity and overcoming the high complexity and cost is of great importance in clinical HCC diagnosis and curative effect evaluation. Herein, an electrochemical immunosensor was constructed by integrating NS-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) with Fe2O3 (Fe2O3@NS-CDs) for the quantitative analysis of AFP. The NS-CDs offered the advantages of good biocompatibility, being rich in functional groups, especially unreduced –COOH, and a large specific surface area, leading to a large number of active sites with good AFP anchoring ability. Owning good electrical conductivity of Fe2O3, the synergistic and electronic effects were achieved by integrating with NS-CDs, which promoted the electron transfer on the surface of the electrode, facilitated capturing antibodies, and generated effective electrochemical signals. Under optimal conditions, the electrochemical immunosensor displayed an ultra-low detection limit (16.8 fg mL−1) in a broad range of 5–1 × 104 pg mL−1. The detection performance of the sensor was improved more than 2 times by integrating NS-CDs, with outstanding selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability. This study provides some constructive insights for the development of promising nanomaterials and methods to promote progress in early diagnostics.

(a) UV–vis spectra of the reaction system in the presence of OPD, OPD + H2O2, Fe2O3@NS-CDs + OPD, and Fe2O3@NS-CDs + OPD + H2O2, (b) CV and (c) EIS spectra of Bare/GCE, NS-CDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE in solution with 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M of KCl at a rate of 50 mV s−1. (d) CV and (e) EIS of bare/GCE, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/GCE, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/GCE, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/AFP/GCE. (f) DPV peak current of Fe2O3/GCE, Fe2O3/Ab/BSA/GCE, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE and Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/AFP/GCE.

(a) CV of Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrode in solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl with the following scan rates: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 mV s−1; (b) corresponding plots of the peak current vs. the square root of the scan rate. (c) CV of Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrode in pH 7.4 PBS (containing 50 ng mL−1 AFP) with the following scan rates: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 mV s−1, (d) corresponding plots of the peak current vs. square root of scan rate.

(a) Electrochemical detection of AFP signal responses to different concentrations of AFP from 5 pg mL−1 to 1 × 104 pg mL−1. (b) Calibration curves of response current towards the logarithm of AFP concentration. (c) DPV response signals about the selectivity of immunosensors for AFP within mixture (100 ng mL−1 interfering substances with 10 ng mL−1 of AFP) and other proteins 100 ng mL−1 of IgG, CEA, BSA, Glu, and AA. (d) Repeatability of the same Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE sensor over 10 measurements for 10 ng mL−1 of AFP. (e) Reproducibility of the detection of 10 ng mL−1 of AFP by 5 different electrodes. (f) DPV current responses about the stability of the immunosensor with 17 days.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a vital biomarker, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is closely associated with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), whose ultrasensitive determination is essential to early diagnosis of HCC and sequential reduction of morbidity and mortality. Especially, the quantitative detection of AFP with high accuracy and ultra-sensitivity and overcoming the high complexity and cost is of great importance in clinical HCC diagnosis and curative effect evaluation. Herein, an electrochemical immunosensor was constructed by integrating NS-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) with Fe2O3 (Fe2O3@NS-CDs) for the quantitative analysis of AFP. The NS-CDs offered the advantages of good biocompatibility, being rich in functional groups, especially unreduced –COOH, and a large specific surface area, leading to a large number of active sites with good AFP anchoring ability. Owning good electrical conductivity of Fe2O3, the synergistic and electronic effects were achieved by integrating with NS-CDs, which promoted the electron transfer on the surface of the electrode, facilitated capturing antibodies, and generated effective electrochemical signals. Under optimal conditions, the electrochemical immunosensor displayed an ultra-low detection limit (16.8 fg mL−1) in a broad range of 5–1 × 104 pg mL−1. The detection performance of the sensor was improved more than 2 times by integrating NS-CDs, with outstanding selectivity, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability. This study provides some constructive insights for the development of promising nanomaterials and methods to promote progress in early diagnostics.

Scheme.1 Schematic illustration for the fabrication process of the electrochemical immunosensor based on Fe2O3@NS-CDs for the detection of AFP.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis and timely treatment of HCC are exceptionally significant. It was reported that HCC is the third leading cause of cancer-related mortality [1–3]. The earlier stage diagnoses are crucial for improving the care and survival rate [4,5]. Therefore, the accuracy of examining the characteristic tumor marker AFP is a prerequisite, because AFP is the earliest and most widely used blood biomarker for screening HCC; the serum concentration of AFP in healthy adults is less than 10 μg L−1, while 80 % of HCC patients have elevated AFP. The international academic community recommends limiting the reference value for AFP to 20 μg L−1 [5]. In recent years, multiple methods, including ELISA [6], radioimmunoassay [7], electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECL) [8], fluorescence [9], and electrochemistry [10], have been used for AFP detection with excellent results. However, most of them are inadequate for HCC screening, not sensitive enough to address the need for early diagnosis, and are often constrained by potential radiation damage and high medical costs [11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for more effective and accuracy approaches to affordable HCC screening that have the potential to identify an individual's potential HCC risk before the onset of symptoms, especially when using simple blood tests [12]. The electrochemical immunosensors rely on antigen-antibody binding, exhibited unique effective advantages for their intriguing merits, including miniaturization, rapid response, simple operation, high accuracy, and stability, have gained significant attention, which could provide qualitative and quantitative detection for AFP [13].

For example, Sun et al. [14] reported a dual-signal output ratiometric electrochemical immunosensor using sea urchin-like PdCuNi as the capture substrate and Co/Ni MOF-Au as the capture probe for detecting the AFP with a large linear range of 0.01–200 ng mL−1 and an LOD of 3.16 pg mL−1. Li et al. [15] developed a portable label-free electrochemical paper analysis device using Cu/Cu2S@NC@Au as a signal amplifier for ultrasensitive AFP detection with a linear range of 0.005–50 ng mL−1 and LOD of 0.62 pg mL−1.

For the design of electrochemical immunosensors, the nanomaterials have been employed in electrochemical immunosensors due to their inherent advantages including large specific surface area, increased number of interfaces, and reduced diffusion length. The precious metal nanoparticles, transition metal sulfides, and semiconductor quantum dots have been utilized for quantitative detection of tumor markers [16,17]. However, the high cost, low conductivity, and toxicity curtailed their extensive application. In comparison, iron oxides (Fe2O3) are a typical class of α-semiconductors [18], which were widely used as multifunctional biomedical materials [19] for their high catalytic activity [20], large specific surface area, chemical stability, good biocompatibility [21], high abundance, and low cost [22]. To promote the conductivity of single-component Fe2O3, modification of Fe2O3 surfaces with CDs was adopted with the availability of the synergistic effect of Fe2O3 and CDs [23]. Simultaneously, CDs demonstrated excellent electronic properties, a large specific surface area, biocompatibility [24], chemical stability [21], and their unique advantages of high affinity, facilitating the immobilizing the antibody. Dual-doped (N, S) allows synergistic coupling effects between heteroatoms, generating more defect sites and surface functional groups, generating more active sites, promoting charge-electron transfer at the interface, and bringing better electrical conductivity [25,26]. Many hydrophilic functional groups on CDs’ surfaces and edges, such as amino (-NH2), hydroxyl (-OH), and carboxyl groups (-COOH), provide active and bonding sites, further improving the charge transfer rate and stability of composites and promoting electrode-electrolyte interactions [27–29]. Hybridizing CDs with Fe2O3 can improve the electrical conductivity and electrochemical activity, depending on their synergistic effects [30], which facilitating the enhancement of the specific surface area [23]. More importantly, constructing an electrochemical immunosensor by integrating CDs with Fe2O3, is beneficial for amplifying detection sensitivity for the detection of tumor markers, attesting to their broad promising future and invaluable real application potential.

In this study, Fe2O3@NS-CDs nanocomposites were fabricated as ideal electrochemical immunosensors by a facile solvothermal process. The synthesis of Fe2O3 nanoparticles was optimized by integrating NS-CDs in refined nano-size and further creating a hydrophilic surface as a substrate facilitating capturing more antibodies. The Fe2O3 integrating NS-CDs not only enhanced the electrical conductivity more than 2-fold compared to single-component Fe2O3, but also possessed oxidase-like catalytic activity, which resulted in excellent redox ability. The high affinity brought by NS-CDs magnified the capturing ability for the antibody, and subsequently generated an electrical signal; the evolution of peak current can reflect the concentration of the target molecule accurately. Finally, the electrochemical immunosensor was used for clinical serum analysis, indicating its application potential in the clinical diagnosis of HCC. Based on these, a new-type NS-CDs doping metallic oxide electrochemical immunosensor has been fabricated for AFP determination (Scheme 1 ). Especially, a precise, powerful, cost-effective, and valuable test tool for the early screening of AFP was established; the testing performance can be comparable to a clinical test and even inspires the design and construction of an advanced precise sensor for the early and accurate diagnosis of various diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NS-CDs and Fe2O3@NS-CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: NS-CDs were synthesized by using the one-step solvothermal method by using glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as precursors. Briefly, 0.0257 g (0.0835 mM) of GSH and 0.0181 g (0.167 mM) of PPD were dissolved in a mixture with 7.5 mL of ultrapure water and 7.5 mL of ethanol. After stirring for 10 min, the mixture was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then the autoclave was heated at 160 °C in an oven for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the dark purple product was collected and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Fe2O3@NS-CDs was prepared according to the reported method with slight modification [31]. Briefly, 0.4 g of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 16 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred thoroughly. Subsequently, 1 g of sodium acetate surfactant and 0.4 g of PEG 4000 were introduced to produce a homogeneous solution under stirring. Then 7.5 mL of NS-CDs were added, and the mixture kept stirring for 1 h. After adequately mixing, the resultant solution was transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down naturally, the product was then collected by centrifugation and washed three times with water and ethanol alternately. The Fe2O3@NS-CDs product was obtained by drying at 80 °C in the oven. The Fe2O3 was synthesized by using a similar approach to evaluate the catalytic performance in comparison.

Prior to surface modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with alumina powders by particle sizes of 1.5, 0.5, and 0.05 μm in sequence and further rinsed with ethanol and ultrapure water thoroughly. Fe2O3@NS-CDs were dispersed in deionized water and ethanol and sonicated for 60 min to form a 4 mg mL−1 homogeneous suspension. Then, 6 μL of the above-dispersed solution was dropped on the GCE and dried at room temperature naturally. The electrode was incubated overnight in a refrigerator at 4 °C after dropping 6 μL of 10 μg mL−1 of antibody solution. The 1% of BSA was added dropwise and dried naturally to the enclosure of non-specific active sites. Subsequently, the electrode surface was rinsed by 0.01 M pH = 7.4 PBS buffer solution. The electrode was then modified with different concentrations of AFP antigen by incubating at 37 °C for 30 min to evaluate the sensing performance of the electrochemical immunosensor.

All electrochemical measurement processes were performed by an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) in a three-electrode system with a GCE as the working electrode, a platinum sheet as the counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The electrochemical behavior of the modified electrode was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe. CV was measured between the potential windows of −0.4 and 1 V. The scanning rate was 50 mV s−1. EIS was carried out in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to record the electrocatalytic behavior of the modified electrodes on the analytes within a potential window of −0.1–0.6 V. The electrolyte was 0.1 M pH = 7.4 PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AFP in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were collected from healthy adult volunteers, and all processes were handled in accordance with the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals [32]. All procedures were conducted legally and approved by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Normal University for research ethics. The practicability of the probe was tested by the standard addition method. The 1 mg mL−1 of AFP antigen solution was diluted by the serum samples to attain gradient concentrations (0, 0.5, 2, 4, 20, 25, 30, 35 ng mL−1) of the AFP antigen solution. Firstly, the AFP serum samples was dropped on the Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA electrodes individually and further treated by incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, then a DPV test was performed subsequently. To verify the accuracy and practicability of the proposed method, the same serum samples were tested and compared with the clinical techniques ELISA and ECL.

The design principle and fabrication process of the electrochemical immunosensor were demonstrated in Scheme 1. The proposed immunosensor was composed of the novel immunoprobe and the antifouling substrate. Initially, Fe2O3 was utilized as an oxidant, Fe(III) was controllably fabricated, and relied on the multivalent cation substitution strategy; part of Fe(III) entered the solution by cation exchange at ambient temperature, and the remaining Fe(III) was integrated with NS-CDs, which facilitated the electron transfer and further promoted their good electrical conductivity. On the basis of the inherent nature of the Fe(III) to Fe(II) conversion, this property allowed for the application to the Fenton-like reaction. After surface modification by Ab, the immunoprobe for AFP electrochemical measurement was obtained. The BSA was employed as a protective film to hinder the electronic transfer, optionally by screening the non-specific active site, and ensured the specificity of the detection. The Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA electrodes can be employed to monitor AFP. In addition, NS-CDs presented rich –NH2, –OH, and –COOH, which provided a net negative charge and improved surfacial hydration. With water retention ability and outstanding biocompatibility, the abundant hydrophilic groups provided abundant electron-rich –OH, which favored electrostatic enrichment for fixing the Ab molecules and improvement of electrical transfer for sensing AFP accurately.

To testify the successful fabrication of Fe2O3@NS-CDs and modifiability, different characterization was employed. The crystal structure and phase of Fe2O3@NS-CDs were analyzed by powder XRD (Fig. 1 a) and further compared with precursors Fe2O3 and NS-CDs. The broad diffraction peak centered at 26.38° of NS-CDs confirmed the presence of partial graphitic structures in the amorphous carbon phase as well as in the carbon cores of the NS-CDs. The diffraction peaks of Fe2O3 located at 24.14°, 35.61°, 43.52°, 54.09°, 57.43°, 63.99°, and 71.94° corresponded to the (012), (110), (202), (116), (122), (300), and (1010) lattice planes, respectively, which matched well with the crystalline plane rhombohedral crystal phase of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS #33–0664), indicating the successful synthesis of Fe2O3 [33]. The diffraction peaks of Fe2O3@NS-CDs at 26.38° corresponded well to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite structure (JCPDS #41–1487) [34]. Further, characteristic diffraction peaks of Fe2O3@NS-CDs at 35.61°, 57.43°, and 63.99° were in accordance with the (110), (122), and (300) crystal planes of α-Fe2O3, respectively. Therefore, the interaction of NS-CDs in the Fe2O3 lattice and the effective integration of NS-CDs on Fe2O3 can be confirmed [31].

The morphology and size of NS-CDs were analyzed by TEM. The spherical NS-CDs with good mono-dispersibility can be observed; crystal stripes with the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm were found, which corresponded to the (112) plane of graphite carbon (Fig. S1) [35]. Furthermore, the histogram of particle size distribution indicated the diameters can be found in the scale of 0.4–2.0 nm, with the average particle size of 1.44 nm ± 0.04 nm (Fig. S2). The SEM images of Fe2O3 and Fe2O3@NS-CDs nanocomposites with similar typical raspberry-shaped morphology were presented (Fig. 1b and c). Fe2O3 nanospheres (Fig. 1b, Fig. S3) exhibited dispersed states and uniform size with the average diameters of 134 nm ± 14 nm. Raspberry structure particles exhibit an appearance of the surface, which can be recognized as the agglomerate, whose total surface area is equal to the sum of individual surfaces [36]. Notably, after integrating with the NS-CDs in the synthesis process, the size of Fe2O3@NS-CDs (Fig. 1c) was restricted to 50 nm, with an average size of 55 nm ± 6 nm (Fig. S4), but the raspberry morphology was reserved, providing the possibility to increase the specific surface area [37]. The results showed that the Fe2O3@NS-CDs binary nanocomposites were effectively synthesized and rationalized with smaller size and fine morphology by the doping NS-CDs, ensuring the improvement of Fe2O3@NS-CDs in catalysis. The EDS of Fe2O3@NS-CDs clearly showed the major constituents in the nanocomposites, including Fe, O, C, N, and S (Fig. S5), further verifying the successful synthesis of Fe2O3@NS-CDs. Ultimately, these results evidenced that the NS-CDs affected the construction of raspberry-shaped Fe2O3 NPs and achieved the preferable nanostructure.

FTIR spectroscopy was utilized to analyze the chemical bonding and functional groups of NS-CDs, Fe2O3, and Fe2O3@NS-CDs samples. As portrayed in Fig. 1d, the strong peak at 3430 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H stretching vibration [38]. The typical stretching mode of CO of three samples can be determined at 1650 cm−1 [39]. The absorption at 1510 cm−1 was considered to be that of N–H [40], and the sharp peak at 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–N stretching [41]. These results confirmed that N doping caused the distribution of a large number of –NH2, –COOH, and –OH groups on the surface of NS-CDs, made it well hydrophilic and easy to surface modify, and ensured the possibility of coordination with antibodies. The stretching vibrational band observed at 600 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of Fe–O, confirming its main contents [42]. Compared with NS-CDs and Fe2O3, the vibration of CO and C–N of Fe2O3@NS-CDs did not shift, just emerging the intensity and shape change significantly. Further, the O–C–C at 1037 cm−1 and Fe–O peak at 600 cm−1 inferred to the contents of Fe2O3 [43], verifying the synthesis of Fe2O3. The N–H stretching vibration peak of NS-CDs located at 1510 cm−1 disappeared after doping with NS-CDs and the formation of Fe2O3@NS-CDs, indicating the key coordination site was N–H, manifested by the NS-CDs combined with Fe2O3 by the N–H bond. The presence of characteristic peaks of Fe2O3 and functional groups of NS-CDs in the composites demonstrated the successful synthesis of Fe2O3@NS-CDs nanocomposites.

The chemical states and electronic structures of Fe2O3@NS-CDs and bonding states were further validated by XPS analysis. The full spectrum of Fe2O3@NS-CDs manifested the presence of Fe (12.3%), O (33.32%), C (51.51%), N (1.85%), and S (1.01%) elements (Fig. S6). The typical peak of C 1s can be categorized into three peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the CC/C–C, C–N/C–O, and –CO/-COOH groups, respectively (Fig. 1e) [44–46]. The N 1s peak deconvoluted at 400.2 eV was attributed to the N–(C)3 (Fig. 1f), indicating the successful N doping CDs [45]. The prominent peaks centered at 530.2 eV [47], 531.7 eV [48], and 533.1 eV [49] in the O 1s region were assigned to the Fe–O–Fe, Fe–OH, and S–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1g). In addition, the peak at 164.1 eV can be assigned to S 2p3/2 (Fig. 1h), confirming the S doping CDs exactly [50]. Typical satellite peaks of Fe2O3 appeared in high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p, two characteristic peaks corresponding to Fe3+ centered at 711.5 eV (Fe 2p3/2) [51] and 724.6 eV (Fe 2p1/2) (Fig. 1i) [52]. Two oscillating satellite peaks of Fe (II) at 716.1 eV and 729.4 eV as well as two oscillating satellite peaks of Fe (III) at 720.7 eV and 733.9 eV can be determined (Fig. 1i). The two peaks with binding energies of 713.7 eV and 728.4 eV are classified as Fe (III), indicating Fe (II) and Fe (III) coexist on the surface of the material. These results manifested their conclusion consistent with FTIR, confirming the synthesis and abundant surfacial groups of Fe2O3@NS-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the Fe2O3@NS-CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-like catalytic activity played a crucial role in the redox electrochemical process; thus, the oxidase-like catalytic activity of Fe2O3@NS-CDs was first investigated (Fig. 2 a). The electrochemical properties of Fe2O3, NS-CDs, and Fe2O3@NS-CDs were evaluated by using CV techniques. And further fabrication and modification of electrochemical immunosensors were optimized and evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, both the NS-CDs and Fe2O3 exhibited well-defined redox peaks. The electrocatalytic characteristics of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE were assessed by comparing their CV curves. After integration, the modified Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE exhibited prominent redox peaks with an ascending trend and maximum peak currents compared with both Fe2O3/GCE and NS-CDs/GCE, manifesting that the introduction of NS-CDs improved the conductivity evidently and conveyed the collaborative efficiency enhancement on electro-conductivity. The EIS was also performed to evaluate the interfacial properties of various modification electrodes, including bare GCE, NS-CDs/GCE, Fe2O3/GCE and Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE. The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) is tantamount to the diameter of the prepared electrode in each step (Fig. 2c); the NS-CDs/GCE showed a smaller Nyquist impedance half-circle diameter than bare GCE, suggesting the good conductivity of NS-CDs. The smaller charge transfer Rct of Fe2O3/GCE than NS-CDs suggested that Fe2O3 can act as an additional channel for [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- electron transfer, improving the electron transfer capability and reducing the resistivity. Notably, the smallest Rct attained by Fe2O3@NS-CDs implied the electron transfer capability was improved by the synergistic effect between the Fe2O3 and NS-CDs, resulting in a significant reduction of Rct, which facilitated the electrochemical conductivity. The synergistic effect of Fe2O3@NS-CDs composites accelerated the electron transfer on the electrode surface and ensured the potential application to be used as an electrochemical sensing probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the construction of the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Consequently, the fabrication process of the biosensor was meticulously evaluated by the utilization of CV and EIS. A comprehensive insight into the evolution of the electrochemical immunosensor's construction can be gleaned. Initially, bare GCE exhibited a pair of characteristic redox peaks, indicating its inherent electrochemical activity. After the modification with Fe2O3@NS-CDs, the redox peak current illustrated an upward trend, indicating effective modification on the electrode surface. After incubation with Ab, the current signal of Fe2O3@NS-CDs was significantly declined (Fig. 2d), reflecting efficient deposition of the Ab blocking layer with insulated conductivity, further verifying the successful binding of the Ab on the Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrode. And the modification of BSA hindered the electron transfer ulteriorly with the evident decline of the redox peak current, owing to the sufficient coverage of the non-conductive bio-substances. Finally, the peak current continuously decreased with the incubation of the AFP, on account of the highly specific immune recognition between AFP and its Ab, further hindering electron transfer, achieving the modification and generation of the ultimate electrochemical immunosensor Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/AFP/GCE. Subsequently, the construction course was further identified by EIS for qualifying the electrode surface properties (Fig. 2e). The conductivity of Fe2O3@NS-CDs was increased, and the semicircular diameter, after modification by Ab, impressively, the Rct displayed an ascending trend by progressive immobilization of Ab, manifesting that Ab was modified and combined with Fe2O3@NS-CDs, further increasing the impedance and hindering the electron transfer on the electrode surface. The semicircular diameter of the curve was further increased, implying the signals were further weakened by modification of BSA, impeding electronic transfer as a non-conductive protein, implying BSA closed the non-specific active site successfully. The impedance of the curve after modification by AFP was higher than that of BSA modification, indicating that the antigen had been successfully modified. The congruity between the findings from both CV and EIS was compelling, providing robust affirmation of the successful and methodical manufacture of the biosensor. The construction of the Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrochemical sensor was successfully validated by the above results. To further assess the sensing ability, the detection performance of the electrochemical immunosensor was evaluated by comparing the superior electrocatalytic effect of electrode materials on AFP. The electrocatalytic activities of Fe2O3@NS-CDs and Fe2O3-modified GCE on AFP were compared by using the DPV technique in 0.1 M pH = 7.4 PBS containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl, and materials were set as a concentration of 4.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 2f). As illustrated, there is a certain difference in peak current of DPV between Fe2O3/GCE and Fe2O3/Ab/BSA/AFP/GCE, Fe2O3 exhibited the trend for sensing performance on AFP. However, Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE and Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/AFP/GCE exhibited increased current differences significantly; the results manifested Fe2O3@NS-CDs’ superior identifying capacity, which can be attributed to the synergies by modification NS-CDs.

The electroactive surface area of modified working electrode is a key characteristic that affects electrochemical performance. Fig. 3 a showed CV curves for the Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE collected in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl solution to varying scan rates in the range of 10–100 mV s−1. The redox current of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE increased with the rising of the scanning rate. The oxidation or reduction currents were fitted, and a linear relationship was obtained between v1/2 and the corresponding anodic or cathodic currents (Fig. 3b). The effective electrochemically active surface area of the modified electrode surface was calculated according to the Randles-Sevcik equation. The calculation equation is as follows:
(1)
I p
=
(
2.69 ×
10 5
)
n
3 / 2
A C
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2
where Ip is the anodic peak current (μA); n is the number of transferred electrons (n = 1); A is the active surface area of the electrode in cm2; C is the concentration, 5 × 10−3 M; D is the diffusion coefficient (D = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s) and v is the scanning rate V s−1.

The electrochemically active surface area of Fe2O3/GCE and Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE was calculated to be 0.04505 cm2 (Figs. S7a and S7b) and 0.05924 cm2 (Fig. 3a and b). respectively, the effective surface area of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE was increased by 0.31 times, thus providing more binding sites for AFP during electrochemical detection, which facilitated the assessment of AFP quantitatively. The CV curves of AFP on Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/GCE were tested in PBS buffer solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl at scan rates of 10–100 mV s−1, to further understand the reaction dynamics of AFP on Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE (Fig. 3c). The peak redox current (Ip) of AFP on the modified electrode increased regularly with the scan rate. Simultaneously, the anodic peak shifted positively and the cathodic peak shifted negatively as the scan rate rose, implying the intense dependent correlation that exists between the Ip and scan rate. A significant linear relationship between both the maximum anodic current (Ipa), cathodic current (Ipc), and the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 3d), with corresponding linear equations of Ipa (μA) = 5.1939v1/2+5.6680 (R2 = 0.996) and Ipc (μA) = −4.149v1/2–9.4917 (R2 = 0.996), indicating that the electrochemical reaction on the surface of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE complied with the diffusion-controlled redox process [53].

To regulate the accuracy and reliability of detection, as well as the detection efficiency, the correlated analytical conditions were optimized by modulating the electrolyte solution pH and electrode incubation time before the evaluation of electrochemical detection. The electrochemical reaction of 50 ng mL−1 AFP on Fe2O3@NS-CDs/Ab/BSA/GCE at different pH conditions was investigated by DPV, owing to the buffer pH directly affecting the charges of target biomolecules, particularly amphoteric immune proteins, thereby causing the effect of interfering substances. Thus, the regulation of pH is essential for maintaining the analytical reproducibility and accuracy. The effectiveness of Fe2O3@NS-CDs composites for the detection of the same concentration of AFP was tested at different pH (6.4–7.8). In the pH range of 6.4–7.4, the peak current increased, accompanied with the rising pH, while it declined as the pH descended from 7.4 to 7.8, and the maximum can be determined at pH = 7.4 (Fig. S8a). The variation can be attributed to the direct relation of the pH to the biological function of the linked proteins. Therefore, the pH 7.4 was determined as the optimal test condition for the detection of AFP. To estimate the effect of incubation time on the modified electrode, the incubation time was also evaluated to avoid an inapposite incubation time caused by the reduction of electrochemical performance. As delineated in Fig. S8b, the modified electrode with 50 ng mL−1 AFP incubating for 30 min exhibited the optimum performance; therefore, 30 min was chosen as the incubation time for the subsequent test.

The constructed Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrochemical sensor was used for quantitative AFP detection by testing DPV under optimal conditions with progressively increasing the AFP concentration (ranging from 5 to 1 × 104 pg mL−1). As portrayed in Fig. 4 a, a series of orderly decreasing current responses can be observed with rising AFP concentration, indicating the inversely proportional relationship between DPV current and AFP concentrations. And the excellent linear correlation can be determined with a linear regression equation of y = −4.3315x + 39.1034 (R2 = 0.998) over the range of 5–1 × 104 pg mL−1 (Fig. 4b), with the limit of detection (LOD) of 16.8 fg mL−1 (based on 3σ/K). The comparison between this proposed method and reported methods was performed and listed in Table S1; the large concentration scale and the ultralow limit manifested the proposed electrochemical sensor was comparable or even surpassed reported strategies.

To further validate the exceptional quality of the Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE electrochemical sensor, the reproducibility, repeatability, interference immunity, and stability were investigated consecutively. Generally, the presence of non-target biomolecules in serum samples interfered with the assay results. Relatively similar proteins and common biomolecules that existed in serum were chosen as interfering substances to assess the selectivity of the electrochemical immunosensor. Further, to evaluate the ability of the electrochemical method to determine AFP in complex serum samples, the above molecules (IgG, CEA, BSA, Glu, and AA) were added to a solution containing 10 ng mL−1 AFP. The concentrations of interfering substances were set 10 times higher than that of AFP and then tested individually. As portrayed in Fig. 4c, no significant interference signal was found even with 10-fold higher concentration than AFP, indicating the excellent capability of anti-interference of Fe2O3@NS-CDs. The repeatability of the Fe2O3@NS-CDs modified electrode was subsequently investigated (Fig. 4d). Under the optimized experimental conditions, the response signal of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE retained 98.6% of the original value after 10 cycles of 10 ng mL−1 AFP, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.46%, implying the reusability. The reproducibility was evaluated by testing five independent modified electrodes with the same procedure for detecting 10 ng mL−1 AFP under the same conditions (Fig. 4e); the results manifested the outstanding reproducibility of the modified electrode with an RSD of 0.05 %, which ensured the reliability of this strategy and Fe2O3@NS-CDs sensor. The stability of the Fe2O3@NS-CDs electrode was further examined by using the DPV method (Fig. 4f). The results revealed the electrochemical sensors possessed superior stability, could be well retained at 4 °C for 17 days with only a 0.55% decrease in the response current value, further verified the dependability of the sensor, and facilitated the promising application in the real test. The above results substantiated the good immunity to interference, repeatability, reproducibility, and stability, ascertaining that the proposed sensor could be used in precise tests for practical applications.

To illustrate the application of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE in biomedical analysis, we electrochemically analyzed the AFP content in human serum samples via standard addition method. The significant current response of Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE to AFP in serum with different amounts of AFP can be observed, indicating that Fe2O3@NS-CDs/GCE was competent to the detection of AFP in serum (Fig. 5 ); the recoveries of 97.62%–101.09% with RSD 2.29%–5.5% were obtained, manifesting the excellent practicability of Fe2O3@NS-CDs. To verify the effective sensing performance of Fe2O3@NS-CDs sensor, the same samples verified by the clinical methods ELISA and ECL were carried out and estimated under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, the tested results by the proposed method and clinical technique almost equated, indicating the promising feasibility of the electrode for the detection of AFP in real samples.

In summary, this study successfully developed a novel AFP electrochemical immunosensor based on a binary nanocomposite Fe2O3@NS-CDs. The integrating NS-CDs with Fe2O3 achieved a smaller size and larger specific surface area, which provided adequate active sites with a synergistic effect, proving their conductivity. Not only the electron transfer rate was promoted, the abundant superficial groups amplified the capacity for capturing AFP antibodies, which provided and enhanced the electrochemical signal effectively, thereby raised the sensitivity and selectivity of AFP detection. Under the optimized conditions, the sensor had excellent analytical performance, achieved sensitive detection of AFP in a linear range (5–1 × 104 pg mL-1) with an LOD (16.8 fg mL−1). In addition, the sensor can also resist the interference of several other tumor markers and common biomolecules in serum and exhibited good repeatability, reproducibility, and acceptable stability. The developed electrochemical immunosensor exhibited comparable performance in the detection of AFP levels in serum samples with the clinical test techniques ELISA and ECL and revealed the advantages of prompt response time, reliability, and high cost-effectiveness. This immunization sensing strategy opened up a new direction for the detection of AFP biomarkers in the clinical diagnosis and monitoring of HCC and has potential application prospects. It was hoped to provide positive help for promoting the development of early cancer diagnosis and screening.
